This edition is self-published, reflecting Rutherford’s commitment to preserving local history. It is ideal for genealogists, local historians, and collectors of New Zealand regional publications. title: Gold Rush NZ Goldfields Tales & Traditions Gordon Ell Historical Mining Book
body:
Gold Rush

Gold Rush by Gordon Ell is a captivating exploration of New Zealand’s goldfields, blending historical accounts with rich storytelling to illuminate the lives, traditions, and landscapes shaped by the gold mining era. This book offers readers a vivid journey through the country’s most storied mining regions, from the first discoveries to the cultural legacy left behind.

Ell’s work is notable for its accessible style and depth of research. He chronicles the experiences of prospectors and diggers, including figures like Charles Ring, Gabriel Read, and Bill Fox, whose efforts sparked gold rushes that transformed entire communities. The book also highlights the diverse makeup of the diggers — from Chinese miners to Aborigines — and the cosmopolitan nature of the goldfields.

The recovery techniques are detailed with clarity, covering everything from pick and pan methods to quartz mining and the cyanide process. These sections provide insight into the ingenuity and labor that defined the era. Ell also delves into the social fabric of goldfield life, exploring themes like law and order, hospitality, and the unique characters who became legends in their own right.

title: NZ Gold Mining History Swiftly Flows The Arrow Arrowtown & District John Thomson
body:
Swiftly Flows the Arrow

Swiftly Flows the Arrow by John Bell Thomson is a richly detailed historical account of Arrowtown and its surrounding district, capturing the essence of New Zealand’s gold mining heritage. This book offers a compelling narrative of the region’s development, from its early settlement through the gold rush era and into its enduring legacy as a cultural and historical landmark.

Thomson’s storytelling blends archival research with vivid descriptions of the people, places, and events that shaped Arrowtown. The book explores the lives of miners, settlers, and local figures who contributed to the town’s unique character. It also delves into the environmental and social changes brought about by the gold rush, offering readers a comprehensive view of the district’s transformation.

Included in the context is reference to Macetown, a nearby historic gold mining settlement, and its connection to Arrowtown. The book complements other regional histories such as King Wakatipu and highlights figures like Gilbert Rees, founder of Arrowtown, whose legacy is woven throughout the narrative.

An Historical Account Of Dunedin Musicians & Musical Societies From The Beginning Of The Settlement To 1864

Margaret Campbell’s detailed account captures the evolution of musical life in the settlement, highlighting the contributions of musicians, choirs, bands, and touring performers who shaped the city’s cultural identity.  

The book provides a vivid narrative of how music was woven into the fabric of early Dunedin society, from church services and community gatherings to formal concerts and visiting artists..  

Campbell’s research is grounded in archival materials and personal recollections, making this volume a valuable resource for historians, musicologists, and collectors interested in New Zealand’s cultural development. title: The Woven Universe - Selected Maori Writings by Rev Maori Marsden
body:
The Woven Universe - Selected Maori Writings by Rev Maori Marsden

he Woven Universe brings together for the first time, Maori Marsden’s substantial statements on Māori philosophy, theology and the Māori worldview. The collection includes his seminal essay, God, Man and Universe: A Māori View and an extract is also included from his final seminar delivered at Te Wānanga-o-Raukawa in Ōtaki.


 2003 Edition 

Does have some water damage - no sticky pages though hence low reserve


Rev. Maori Marsden (1924-1993) was a tohunga, scholar, writer, healer, minister and philosopher of the latter part of the twentieth century. From the Tai Tokerau in the north, Maori was both an ordained Anglican minister and a graduate of the whare wānanga, the traditional tribal centre of higher and esoteric learning. He was uniquely placed to explore and explain the frontier between pre-Christian theology, understandings of divinity and the Māori worldview, and his Christian faith and vocation.

Throughout his life, Maori was asked to speak and heal in Māori communities throughout the country and he wrote a substantial amount of material on matters facing the contemporary Māori quest for social justice and the achievement of authentic being.

In his writings, sensitively edited by Te Ahukaramū Charles Royal, Marsden's thoughts venture far and wide and include analyses and views on key concepts in the Māori worldview and knowledge, politics, science (particularly the New Physicists), the Treaty of Waitangi, the legal system and much more.

title: THE MAORIS IN THE GREAT WAR HISTORY OF NZ NATIVE CONTINGENT & PIONEER BATTALION
body:
THE MAORIS IN THE GREAT WAR

This powerful historical volume by James Cowan documents the service of Māori soldiers during World War I, focusing on the New Zealand Native Contingent and the Pioneer Battalion. Published in 1926 by the Māori Regimental Committee and printed by Whitcombe & Tombs in New Zealand, this book offers a rare and detailed account of Māori contributions to the Gallipoli campaign and the Western Front between 1915 and 1918.

Cowan’s writing blends military history with cultural insight, capturing the bravery, resilience, and camaraderie of Māori troops in some of the most grueling theatres of war. The book includes firsthand accounts, regimental movements, and reflections on the unique role of Māori soldiers within the broader ANZAC forces. It also explores the political and social backdrop that led to the formation of the Māori Contingent, including tribal responses to the call for service and the impact of war on Māori communities.

He flew Vampires, Sunderlands & Orions, served in the Congo Uprising, & became a member of the famed RNZAF aerobatic team. The second half of the book explores his transition to commercial aviation,

title: WHAKAIRO MAORI TRIBAL ART BY DAVID SIMMONS CULTURAL HISTORY & CARVING TRADITIONS
body:
WHAKAIRO MAORI TRIBAL ART

Whakairo Maori Tribal Art by David Simmons is a richly illustrated and deeply researched exploration of traditional Māori carving and its cultural significance..

David Simmons, a respected ethnologist and historian, presents the carvings not merely as decorative elements but as visual narratives that preserve whakapapa (genealogy), tribal stories, and spiritual beliefs. The book examines the evolution of Māori art styles, the meanings behind carved motifs, and the role of whakairo in wharenui (meeting houses), waka (canoes), and taonga (treasures). It also explores the influence of tribal identity on carving forms and the protocols surrounding the creation and placement of carved works.

This edition includes extensive tribal histories and regional carving styles with photographs of carved figures, panels, and architectural elements that highlight the craftsmanship and spiritual depth of Māori carving traditions.

body:
THE CHOPPER BOYS

The Chopper Boys by Rex Forrester is a thrilling account of New Zealand’s pioneering helicopter hunting era. This book captures the raw energy, danger, and ingenuity of the men who transformed deer recovery into a high-flying industry. Forrester, a legendary outdoorsman and storyteller, chronicles the rise of commercial venison recovery and the role helicopters played in reshaping the landscape of hunting in Aotearoa.

Set in the rugged backcountry of the 1960s and 70s, the book follows the evolution of deer hunting from ground-based stalking to aerial operations. It explores the development of techniques for spotting, shooting, and retrieving deer carcasses using helicopters, and the emergence of a new breed of hunter—part cowboy, part pilot, all adrenaline. The narrative is packed with real-life stories of daring chopper maneuvers, close calls, and the camaraderie among hunters and crew.

He introduces readers to the “meat hawks,” the “cowboys of the deer squadrons,” and the “jet-age professionals” who helped build a multimillion-dollar industry from the skies
This edition is a softcover with 176 pages. It includes black-and-white photographs that document the helicopters, hunters, and terrain that defined the era.

title: ENGLISH MAORI DICTIONARY BY HM NGATA
body:
English Maori Dictionary by H M Ngata 

This dictionary is the most extensive English to Maori dictionary yet published, offering over 14,500 headwords.

 It is the first major dictionary to be written by Maori for the study of te reo Maori. The work was originated more than thirty years ago by Hori Ngata, and has been compiled by native speakers of the language, including many Ngati Porou kaumatua, with the help of a team of linguists and editors.

 Based primarily on the Ngati Porou dialect, the dictionary embraces a wide range of tribal usages and is accessible to students at a basic level, as well as those with an advanced knowledge of the Maori language. The dictionary has a simple format, providing headwords in English and Maori, with sentences illustrating usage. Commentary and grammatical material have been kept to a minimum; a simply set-out guide illustrates how to use the dictionary.

 Appendices list terms for flora and fauna, points of the compass, major New Zealand towns, the terminology of kinship, and categories of possessive relationships

title: COOKS ARK HISTORY OF ANIMALS ON VOYAGES WITH CAPTAIN COOK TO SOUTH PACIFIC
body:
Cook’s Ark  
By Alison Sutherland  

Cook’s Ark is a compelling account of the animals that accompanied Captain James Cook on his voyages to the South Pacific, with a special focus on New Zealand. Alison Sutherland’s research uncovers a lesser-known aspect of maritime exploration: the role of livestock and exotic species in shaping European understanding of Pacific fauna.  

The book explores the scientific and artistic achievements of Cook’s expeditions, highlighting the contributions of naturalists and illustrators who documented the animals and environments encountered. It also examines the impact of these voyages on the animals themselves, including their fate upon returning to Europe.  

Sutherland’s narrative is enriched by historical records and illustrations, offering insight into how Cook’s journeys influenced perceptions of the Pacific and its biodiversity. The final chapter lists animals introduced to New Zealand as a result of these voyages.
The contents are organized into four parts, covering each of Cook’s three circumnavigations and the legacy of his voyages.

title: Phil Lightband RNZAF Fighter Pilot - Topdresser SIGNED
body:
Earlybird The Story Of Phil Lightband

This rare signed biography captures the pioneering spirit of Phil Lightband, a New Zealand aviation legend whose contributions to aerial photography, float planes, and trans-Tasman flight helped shape the country’s aviation history. Written by Wendy Laurenson and published in 1996, this softcover edition is signed by both Laurenson and Lightband, adding collector appeal for aviation enthusiasts and New Zealand history buffs alike.

Phil Lightband’s journey began with a childhood flight that sparked a lifelong passion for aviation. He later flew solo in light aircraft and became a trailblazer in aerial imaging, using electronic tools to revolutionize how we see the world from above. In 1950, he flew a single-engine aircraft from England to Sydney, marking the start of a career that would span photography, engineering, and environmental innovation.

The book includes 318 pages of rich narrative, historical photographs, and technical insights. It documents Lightband’s role in introducing ski-planes, float planes, and early aerial top-dressing to New Zealand. His work also supported trans-Tasman business commuting and passive solar flight technologies. The biography is both a personal story and a chronicle of aviation development in the region.

title: Moana Maniapoto  A history of our family Hardcovered Book + Genealogy  ROTORUA
body:
Moana Maniapoto  A history of our family

Years ago, my sister Trina and I produced a hardcover book called Under The Apple Tree, printing off 50 copies just for our whānau. It was a collection of stories written with two things in mind — first, to remind Bernie and Nepia’s six children that their parents had a life before our arrival. And second, to capture family stories, whakapapa and photographs for future generations.

 We interviewed Mum, Dad and his siblings. They’re all gone now.

As kids, we grew up knowing our Ngāti Te Rangiita, Tūhourangi, Ngāti Pikiao, Raukawa whakapapa. Then Mum quietly handed over her family tree, captured in glorious calligraphy. There was a collective jaw drop. It turned out that on her English side, Bernie was descended from knights and baronets whose roots were traced back to 1169.

Warena, another of Mum’s former sons-in-law, won top marks at the tangi by reciting her genealogy from memory. One helluva mean feat given every second name on the tree seemed to be Thomas, Guy, or Anne. The Honywood family has its own coat of arms — three hawks and a wolf’s head — and a motto that reads: “Every good thing is from above.”

In 1883, my great-grandfather on Mum’s side, Henry Molloy, left County Antrim in Northern Ireland and sailed to New Zealand on the Victory. After a stint on a sheep run and marriage to an Irish lass named Ellen Connolly, the couple bought the Imperial Hotel in Oamaru. According to his wife, it was sold after Henry had drunk all the profits.... And much more 

Hrdcovered 130 pages hardback

title: Barry Crump Collected Stories 5 Classic Books In 1 Pulished 2017 VG
body:
Barry Crump Collected Stories

This volume brings together five classic tales by Barry Crump, one of New Zealand’s most widely read authors. Known for his storytelling style that captures the humour and grit of the Kiwi outdoorsman, Crump’s work has sold over a million copies since his debut in 1960.

The collected stories include A Good Keen Man, Bullock Creek, Gold And Greenstone, Wild Pork And Watercress, and Puha Road. These tales reflect Crump’s signature themes of bush life, mateship, and the rugged New Zealand landscape. His writing influenced generations and inspired the film Hunt For The Wilderpeople, which was based on Wild Pork And Watercress.

This edition includes an introduction by Kevin Ireland and offers readers a chance to revisit Crump’s most enduring yarns in one volume. The stories span decades of New Zealand life and literature, making this a valuable addition to any collection focused on local heritage and storytelling.

Published posthumously, this hardcover edition is designed to preserve Crump’s legacy and make his work accessible to new readers. It is ideal for those interested in New Zealand literature, bush culture, and the evolution of storytelling in Aotearoa.

title: Stag Doo Big Al Lester Hunting Yarns 233 pages
body:
It's a Bit Rugged, Mate by Big Al Lester New Zealand Hunter 416 pages 

Cardcovered good+++

Once described as 'Barry Crump meets Fred Dagg', 'Big Al' Lester is the modern-day master of the hunting genre.

 'I'm often asked what inspired me to take up writing yarns about the mishaps and mayhem that I have endured while venturing into the New Zealand bush. 'In truth, I am an average to poor hunter who has had more hunting cock-ups than the rest of the country combined

 For every deer I've successfully shot, dozens have escaped only to hide in the bush peering back at me, clearly laughing at my stupidity. I have always managed to see the funny side of the situation and had a good old laugh at myself. I began to wonder if my stuff-ups, with a bit of humour and mayhem thrown in, might just make for good reading - the oddball, unusual, humorous, weird and fun side of hunting - and gave it a go. 

I must have got something right.' - 'Big Al' Lester. It's a Bit Rugged, Mate compiles the biggest and best yarns from Big Al's first three books - plenty of fun for those with a good keen sense of humour and a love for New Zealand's wild outdoors.

title: A photographic History of Motueka Wharf - 100 years by Carol Dawber
body:
A photographic History of Motueka Wharf - 100 years by Carol Dawber

Motueka Wharf: 100 Years

This book by Carol Dawber, in collaboration with the Motueka and Districts Historical Association, chronicles a century of maritime activity at Motueka Wharf. Richly illustrated and deeply researched, it captures the evolution of the wharf from its early days in 1885 through to 2016, highlighting its role in shaping the town’s economy, culture, and identity.

The chapters are organised by era:
- The old wharf 1885–1916  
- The new wharf 1916–1946  
- Changing times 1947–1977  
- Private enterprise 1977–2016  
- Bibliography  
- Index  

Motueka’s port has long been central to the region, supporting passenger services, fishing fleets, and the export of hops, tobacco, timber, and livestock. In 1955 alone, 276 vessels entered the harbour. The book explores the political and environmental challenges faced over the years, including the formation of the Motueka Harbour Board and the wharf’s reconstruction in ferro-cement in 1924.

Published to mark the centennial of the current wharf, this volume documents the ships, workers, and families whose lives were intertwined with the Tasman Bay port.

title: Souvenir Book Of Cookery Recipes 1938 NZ Dry Cleaning Rotorua Howe St & Tips
body:
Souvenir Book of Cookery Recipes – Fourth Edition, July 1938

This vintage cookbook was published in July 1938 as a promotional item by the New Zealand Dry Cleaning Co. Ltd. It introduces their Rotorua branch on Tutanekai Street and includes the company’s head office details at 1, 3, 5 Howe Street, Auckland. The book was distributed with compliments and reflects a unique blend of domestic advice and corporate outreach from the 1930s.

The fourth edition followed three earlier impressions, with a total of 56,000 copies printed between 1934 and 1938. It contains 64 pages of practical recipes and household tips, including sections on soups, fish, meats, cakes, biscuits, jams, sauces, and kitchen measurements. The index is well-organised, making it easy to navigate for cooks of all levels.

A standout feature is the chapter titled “The Romance of the Dry-Cleaning & Dyeing Industry,” which offers historical insight into the company’s operations and marketing approach. This book is a rare example of early 20th-century New Zealand promotional publishing and is of interest to collectors of vintage cookbooks, advertising ephemera, and domestic history.

Hardback 
Pages: 64  
Date: July 1938  
Condition:  Excellent

body:

Scarce book

This historical publication presents a rich chronicle of life in Maungakaramea, drawn from the personal diary of Harriet Watson, spanning from 1886 to 1932. Researched and transcribed by Bethley O’Shea, with layout and design by Maria Quin, the book was published in 2009 and offers a deeply personal window into early settler life in Northland, New Zealand.

Harriet began her diary on her wedding day, and her entries document decades of domestic life, community events, and the evolving landscape of Maungakaramea. The diary was preserved and edited with care by her descendants, including Jean Goodliffe, whose contributions helped bring Harriet’s voice to a wider audience.

The introduction outlines the diary’s journey from handwritten pages to published form, highlighting the collaborative effort to honour Harriet’s legacy. The book includes photographs, genealogical notes, and reflections that illuminate the hardships and joys of pioneer life.

This is a valuable resource for historians, genealogists, and anyone interested in New Zealand’s rural heritage and the lived experiences of women in the late 19th and early 20th centuries.

title: Celebrating Schooling In Coromandel 1873 - 1998
body:
COROMANDEL SCHOOLS

 Over the weekend of 3-5 April 1998 Coromandel celebrated 125 years of schooling in the area. More than 400 former pupils, family members and school staff attended. The ringing of the old Coromandel School bell on the Friday signalled the start of activities which ended on Sunday with a church service, dedication of a commemorative plaque and a tree planting at the area school. Saturday's formalities began with a powhiri, a blessing and the official opening by Thames - Coromandel District Mayor Alasdair Thompson. Displays of memorabilia were of great interest.

In 1873, two public schools were set up in Coromandel township, Kapanga School in a rented room, and Driving Creek in a former private school building. Several private schools already existed, but the passing of the (Auckland) Education Act in 1872 paved the way for new public schools in many areas.

Since that time at least 17 Schools in the northern Coromandel, both public and private, have contributed to the 125 years of Coromandel schooling.

Driving Creek School, at the corner of Kennedy Bay Road and the old Colville Road, closed in 1915. Kapanga School, later renamed Coromandel Public School (in the building now known as Hauraki House) was closed in 1975, when the District High School on Woollams Avenue became Coromandel Area School.

This beautifully compiled spiral-bound guide by Jenny Boyack and John Hornblow offers a journey through 100 significant sites across Aotearoa New Zealand. Blending Christian pilgrimage with cultural and historical reflection, the book invites readers to explore sacred places, memorials, churches, and landscapes that have shaped the nation’s spiritual identity.

title: Samuel Austin Early Wanganui Settler New Zealand Maori Wars 1831 - 1903 NZ Cross
body:
For Gallant Service Rendered The Life & Times Of Samuel Austin

This compelling biography traces the extraordinary life of Samuel Austin, a soldier whose journey spanned continents and conflicts. Beginning with his childhood in Tandragee, Ireland, Austin’s military career launched with the British Army and culminated in his service with the Wanganui Native Contingent during the pursuit of Te Kooti in 1870. The book draws extensively from Austin’s own diary, offering an intimate and vivid account of his experiences during the New Zealand Wars and his later life as a settler in Wanganui.

Barbara Mabbett, Austin’s great-great-niece, brings his story to life with scholarly insight and personal dedication. Her research was inspired by historian James Belich and led her to uncover the many facets of Austin’s life. Mabbett’s background in education and curriculum development adds depth and clarity to this historical narrative.

title: Glenn Turners Century Of Centuries Signed NZ Cricket Book With Match Details
body:
Glenn Turner's Century Of Centuries

This signed edition celebrates one of New Zealand’s greatest cricketing achievements — Glenn Turner’s remarkable feat of scoring 100 centuries across his professional career. Co-authored with Ray Cairns, the book offers a comprehensive and personal account of each century, including match scoreboards, bowling attacks, milestone details, and Turner's own reflections.

Turner’s hundredth century was scored in spectacular fashion at New Road, Worcester, where he also made his first. He reached 311 not out against Warwickshire in less than a day, scoring a century before lunch and another by tea. This milestone capped a glittering career that spanned domestic and international cricket, with matches played in venues across New Zealand, England, the Caribbean, and Australia.

Lavishly illustrated with photographs and run-charts, the book is both a tribute and a detailed archive. It includes chapters dedicated to each century, making it a valuable resource for cricket historians, fans, and collectors. The signed title page adds further appeal for enthusiasts of New Zealand cricket memorabilia.

This edition is published by Hodder and Stoughton, with locations listed in Auckland, London, Sydney, and Toronto. Glenn Turner’s legacy as a batsman and his contribution to New Zealand cricket are preserved in this unique volume.

title: In The Shadow Of The Mountain -  Farming 1920's - Mount Terako Marlborough
body:
In The Shadow Of The Mountain

This evocative collection of rural memoirs by Graeme Robson captures the spirit, grit, and humour of country life in New Zealand’s Amuri region.  The book offers a nostalgic and insightful look into farming life, community resilience, and the natural challenges of living in the shadow of Mount Terako, Marlborough


Robson’s storytelling is rich with personal anecdotes and historical reflections, beginning with his family’s immigration journey aboard the ship Wellington in 1879. The narrative follows generations of settlers, including tales of milking cows before weddings, shearing sheep in snowstorms, and managing the unpredictable manuka scrub. These stories are both intimate and universal, reflecting the experiences of many rural New Zealanders.

The book is structured into themed chapters, each offering a unique lens on country living. From managing livestock and battling snow to remembering neighbours and returning to the mountain, Robson’s prose is warm, humorous, and deeply rooted

body:
Yesteryears Of Rotorua

Yesteryears Of Rotorua presents a rich pictorial record of the city’s architectural and social history, bringing together a wide range of historical images that document Rotorua’s changing landscape. First published in 2022, this volume by Philip Andrews captures the evolution of key buildings, public spaces, and community landmarks, offering readers an engaging visual journey through time. The book highlights the tension between preservation and progress, showing how decisions around demolition, repair, and redevelopment have shaped the modern city.


The book includes a variety of early city council photographs, illustrating shifts from horse‑drawn transport to motor vehicles, changes in bus routes, and the disappearance of once‑familiar commercial buildings. Andrews’ selection of images encourages reflection on what is lost when older structures are removed and how heritage contributes to a city’s identity. The volume is printed in softcover format and contains numerous high‑quality photographs accompanied by informative captions.

title: Prestacon metal construction kit system (~1947-1950) Similar to Meccano + Press
body:
The short-lived Prestacon metal construction kit system (~1947-1950) was superficially similar to the better-established Juneero sets, which had been around since before WW2. Both systems were based around their own special patented punching and bending tool, which was designed to cut tinplated steel in the case of Juneero, and thin aluminium strip in the case of Prestacon.

One of the selling points of the Prestacon system was the tool's array of equally-spaced registration holes (set at quarter-inch ontervals), that let one set end-stops to a range of standard distances ("no measuring!"). For longer pieces of metal there was an extension part that let that the "no measuring" principle still apply to pieces of material that were too big to completely fit onto the base of the tool. A further sophistication was that the spacers were reversible, depending on whether one wanted the bend on a sheet of metal to fit around a given distance (distance plus the thickness of the metal), or inside that distance (distance minus the thickness of the bent metal).

This, of course, assumed that one was using metal of a standard thickness, as supplied by Prestacon.

title: Vintage Mens Microma Swiss LCD Quartz Alarm Watch Dual Display  Retro Timepiece
body:
Microma Swiss LCD Quartz Alarm Watch Vintage Dual Display Blue Face Retro Timepiece

This vintage Microma Swiss LCD Quartz Alarm watch is a classic example of early digital timekeeping design. With its distinctive dual-display layout and bold blue face, this timepiece captures the retro aesthetic of the late 1970s and early 1980s. The left display shows the time, while the right display presents the date, both framed by vertical text detailing its features.

The watch face includes the brand name "Microma Swiss" on the left and "LCD Quartz" with "Alarm" on the right, accompanied by a musical note symbol indicating its alarm functionality. The metallic casing and band add to its vintage appeal, offering a sleek and durable finish that complements the digital interface.

Microma was among the pioneering brands in digital watch technology, and this model showcases the precision and style that made Swiss engineering renowned worldwide. The dual-display format was particularly popular for its clarity and utility, allowing users to view both time and date at a glance. The blue background adds a vibrant contrast to the digital numerals, enhancing readability and visual impact.

Collectors of vintage electronics and retro fashion will appreciate the authenticity and condition of this piece. It’s ideal for display or wear  and represents a unique moment in horological history.

 The watch’s alarm feature and quartz accuracy make it both functional and collectible.

from what I gather it has the same movement as a Longines LCD watch 

Date of manufacture: circa late 1970s to early 1980s

title: The Kaikoura UFOs NZ Pilot Sightings 1978 UFO Encounter by  Bill Startup
body:
The Kaikoura UFOs NZ Pilot Sightings 

The Kaikoura UFOs by Bill Startup is a detailed account of the extraordinary aerial events witnessed off the coast of Kaikoura in December 1978. This hardcover volume documents one of New Zealand’s most widely reported UFO encounters, combining eyewitness testimony, radar data, and photographic evidence to reconstruct the sequence of sightings that captured international attention. The book presents the experiences of airline pilots, crew members, and observers who reported unexplained lights and objects tracking alongside their aircraft during routine flights.

Startup provides a clear narrative of the events as they unfolded, describing how the sightings were recorded on film and confirmed by ground radar operators. The book examines the conditions of the night flights, the reactions of the crew, and the subsequent investigations that attempted to explain the phenomena. It includes analysis of the film footage, diagrams, and maps that help illustrate the flight paths and positions of the objects relative to the aircraft. The author also discusses the media coverage that followed, which brought the Kaikoura sightings into global conversation.


The book includes photographic plates, maps, and supporting documentation. It provides a comprehensive overview of the Kaikoura encounters and the investigations that followed, making it an important reference for those studying the case or exploring New Zealand’s place in UFO history.

Published 1980  Hardback

title: History of the War in the North told by an old Chief of the Ngapuhi Tribe
body:
Old New Zealand by a Pakeha Maori 

A tale of the good old times by a Pakeha Maori F.E.Maning,and a History of the War in the North told by an old Chief of the Ngapuhi Tribe. Judge Maning arrived in New Zealand's shores spluttering, his clothes in ribbons be greeted by an amused crowd of Maoris and Pakehas whom he had hoped to impress. This undignified and hilarious arrival begins the raciest, most humorous account of the rumbustious days 'before governors were invented, and law and justice and all that'. 

The fearless exploits of his adoptive chief who eventually dies murmuring 'how sweet is man's flesh';the plundering raids on enemy villages the sinister magic of the tohunga the Maori version of The Treaty Of Waitangi and the Flag Staff War Maning describes them all with brilliance touched by nostalgia for the passing of good old times.

title: Adventure In New Zealand E J Wakefield Accounts of New Zealand Life 1840's 2 Vol
body:
Adventure In New Zealand E J Wakefield

2 Volume set

It is given to few young men, particularly one not yet twenty, to be part of such a stupendous undertaking as the birth of a nation.
It was fortunate for New Zealand that Edward Jerningham Wakefield, who came from England in the Tory in 1839 as. secretary to his uncle, Colonel-William Wakefield. had a definite literary bent, for he has left us a most colourful (if sometimes one-sided) account of the beginnings of Wellington and the other New Zealand Company settlements.

His official position, inquiring mind, and an urge to see as much of this new land as possible gave young Wakefield unique opportunities to record impressions which are invaluable today.

Such prominent contemporary figures as Governor Hobson (whom he derides as “the Auckland Pasha”), Captain Fitzroy, Lieut. Willoughby Shortland, the Wakefield brothers, early explorers such as Dr Dieffen. bach and Charles Heaphy, the missionary Octavious Hadfield and the notorious Te Rauparaha and many more, pass through his pages.

Jerningham Wakefield’s book has been variously described as “a very effective piece of New Zealand Company propaganda . . . valuable for its colour and vigour . . . a remarkable production for a young man in his early twenties . . . as picturesquely enthralling as it is prejudiced.”
But, whatever its faults, the book (now reproduced by Wilson and Horton Limited, Auckland, in facsimile form*) remains for the modern reader a rich tapestry into which has been woven vivid pictures of almost every conceivable aspect of life in the first few years of the colony.

Softcovered 482 + 546 pages

title: Southland New Zealand & Southern Islands from 1770 to 1829 first published 1907
body:
Murihiku and the Southern Islands  Robert McNab

facsimile edition 

A history of the West Coast Sounds, Foveaux Strait, Stewart Island, The Snares, Bounty, Antipodes, Auckland, Campbell and Macquarie Islands, from 1770 to 1829 first published 1907 in Invercargill, NZ 

A mine of information on early happenings in the South Island of New Zealand, from the arrival of the first European seal hunters in the late 1790s up to 1835, when the explorers of the various nations had come and gone, “Murihiku” is a must for the historically minded reader. Its author, Robert McNab. was born in 1864, the son of a Southland runholder. He graduated MA at Otago University in 1884 and LL.B in 1890. followed the practice of law for six years and, for the rest of his life, divided his time between farming, public life and historical research.
With a modest account of early Southland in mind, he searched the libraries and archives of Tasmania, New South Wales, the United States, Britain, France and Spain for records of early European contacts with his own island. The result was “Murihiku.’ first published in 1905, and now re-issued in facsimile form by Wilson & Horton Ltd.
In the course of his political life. McNab became Minister of Lands and, later, Minister of Justice and Marine. At Richard Seddon’s suggestion. the raw material McNab had collected was published in 1908 as “The Historical Records of New Zealand,” (in two volumes) one of the most useful reference works on the early history of this country.

title: A History Of Ratana The Man The Church And The Political Movement SCARCE
body:
Ratana The Man, the Church, the Political Movement

 By J M Henderson

Hardback 1972 second edition

RATANA

A significant part of the New Zealand social scene, the Ratana Movement attracts the interest of the public each January when the gathering of thousands of members in conference is reported in the press and on television. In Parliament the four Maori seats have been held almost continuously for a third of a century by the "Four Quarters" who are members of the Ratana Church and the Labour Party.

This book tells the story of the Movement, from its origins last century and the prophecies concerning Tahupotiki Wiremu Ratana who rose to fame as a faith-healer in modern times, to the founding of the Ratana Church, the building of the Temple, and the sealing of an alliance between the "Mouthpiece of God" and the first Labour prime minister.

The author discusses the reasons for the rise of the Movement and its place in modern, bi-cultural New Zealand.

This revised edition incorporates new in- formation and shows the depth and range of interest in this charismatic, value-oriented Maori movement.

Legends clung around George Moonlight, from the provenance of his unusual names- it was rumoured he was born on a fine night (Fairweather), abandoned and found in the moonlight; to his reputation as a self-sufficient explorer and gold prospector of uncanny ability.1 

Born in Scotland into the Moonlight family, he arrived in New Zealand via the Californian and Australian goldfields in 1860.2  Moonlight and his cousin Tom headed for the Otago goldfields in 1861 and there was soon news of a rich find near Lake Wakatipu – named Moonlight Creek in his honour. Moonlight’s discovery started a gold rush into the area.

Moonlight’s hallmark was the repeated rapid discoveries of major gold areas, then leaving them for hundreds of diggers. His appearance and dress also attracted notice: tall and muscular, he dressed as a Californian gold miner, in a crimson shirt, buckskin breeches and a maroon sash.

Moonlight’s most remarkable gold discovery came in April 1865 in the Paparoa Range, near Blackball. “Virgin gold lay round the parent reef with ‘quars’ (quartz) still embedded in its large nuggets.” The ravine was named Moonlight Gully (Atarau). About eight tons of gold was eventually obtained from the gully by 100 –150 diggers, with the 87½ oz Victoria nugget found in 1917.

24 pages

title: The Whips are Cracking Letters from Bomber Pilot 75 New Zealand Sqn R.A.F SIGNED
body:
The Whips are Cracking!: Letters from a Bomber Pilot 75 (NZ) Sqn. R.A.F.

Firsthand letters of a New Zealand bomber pilot serving with the renowned 75 NZ Squadron RAF during the Second World War. The narrative traces his early life, his journey toward flying, and the intense operational environment faced by aircrew in Bomber Command. The personal correspondence provides a vivid account of training, deployment, and the emotional weight carried by those flying night missions over Europe. The book opens with reflections on childhood and formative influences before moving into the voyage aboard the Akaroa and the transition to life in England. The pilot describes the culture of the squadron, the camaraderie among airmen, and the constant pressure of operational readiness. His letters capture the atmosphere of bases such as Newmarket and the evolving tactics of German night fighter defences during 1942. The writing conveys both the routine and the danger of missions, offering insight into the technical and human challenges of wartime aviation.

title: Regional History Of Early Rangitikei  The Day Of Striding Out 225 pgs
body:
The Day Of Striding Out In Rangitikei New Zealand Regional History Of Growth And Change

This book explores the story of Rangitikei as a legend brought into reality through the development of a region shaped by landscape, people, and progress. The narrative reflects on the meaning of the name Rangitikei and the long stride from early settlement to a thriving agricultural district. The text describes the transformation from wilderness to a productive area defined by farming, transport routes, and community growth. 

The account highlights the early challenges of sand, swamp, and dense forest and the gradual introduction of bridle tracks, metalled roads, and highways that connected the eighteen hundred square mile region in the lower North Island. The story emphasises the arrival of the railway, the spread of electric power lines, and the influence of internal combustion engines as each new technology reshaped the district

. The book presents the people and places that experienced the Day of Striding Out and the determination required to build a sustainable community.

title: Tales of Papatoetoe The Childhood Years of Lorna Jenks 1938-1953 Double Signed
body:
Anna Hoffmann Tales of Papatoetoe The Childhood Years of Lorna Jenks 1938-1953

I was born Lorna Anne Jenks, in March 1938, under the astrological sign of Pisces.


I was brought up in the South Auckland suburb of Papatoetoe in the forties and fifties.


 This book is dedicated to Papatoetoe.


It is about a time when there was no television - only wireless - and the worst crime was to ride on the ferry or tram and not pay.


Divorces were published in Truth. Women wore hats, gloves and stockings to town. Men wore grey flannel trousers, a sports jacket and tie, and almost always, a hat.


There was no unemployment and no benefits unless you were sick.


Everything closed down on Sundays. Kids climbed trees and played in the streets. Women were barred from public bars. It was a wonderful time!



276 pages with photos

title: Maori Scenes From Early New Zealand Augustus Earle In New Zealand 1820's
body:
Maori Portraits & Scenes From Early New Zealand Augustus Earle In New Zealand

60 plates 

Earle was determined to know more about the Māori, some of whom he had met in Sydney. In October 1827 he sailed on the Governor Macquarie and spent eight months between Hokianga and the Bay of Islands. He was convinced that no native race he had studied on his travels could compare with the New Zealanders, that 'splendid race of men' with 'a natural elegance and ease of manner'. He found that although the Māori were warlike, often cruel, and sometimes treacherous, they were brave and chivalrous and also generous, faithful friends. He admired their physique and could not forgive the missionaries for seeking to hide such naked splendour in European clothing for the sake of modesty and morality. He praised their 'busy enquiring minds', was fascinated by the complexity of their character, impressed by their artistry in carving and by their mastery of the art of warfare. He likened Hongi Hika to a Homeric hero.

Earle painted accurate representations of Māori customs, occasions and domestic scenes, for instance, 'Crying over the bones of a dead chief', 'Warriors presenting trophies of conquest [the heads of their enemies] to their queen', 'A war speech previous to a naval expedition' and 'Slaves preparing food'. The squalor portrayed in the 'Residence of Shulitea [Te Uri-ti]' makes an interesting contrast with the splendid ornamentation depicted in 'Tabooed house'. His pen sketches of Māori people are infused with the human understanding of his fellow men implicit in his written comments.

title: 5th Bombardment Group Heavy   BOMBER BARONS
body:
5th Bombardment Group (5th BG) "Bomber Barons"

U.S. Army Air Force (USAAF)

Background

Established as the 5th Composite Group (5th CG). Later, redesignated 5th Bombardment Reconnaissance Group (5th BRG) then designated 5th Bombardment Group (5th BG). Nicknamed "Bomber Barons" and operated the B-24 Liberator in the South Pacific (SOPAC) comprised of Headquarters Squadron (HQ), 23rd Bombardment Squadron (23rd BS), 31st Bombardment Squadron (31st BS), 72nd Bombardment Squadron (72nd BS) and 394th Bombardment Squadron (394th BS). Nicknamed "Bomber Barons" the motif was a skull with wings with "Kiai O Kalewa" (Hawaiian for "Guardians of the Upper Realm").

Wartime History
The 5th Bombardment Group (5th BG) nicknamed "Bomber Barons" operated in the South Pacific (SOPAC) during World War II. At the start of the Pacific War, assigned to the 7th Air Force (7th AF). In 1943, assigned to the 13th Air Force (13th AF). On June 15, 1944 became part of the Far East Air Force (FEAF) until the end of World War II.

This document is intended to cover the history of the Fifth Bombardment Group from the era immediately preceding WWII, through the war years until V-J Day 1945. It is presented against a summary background of the entire life of the organization

Photographs of her playfulness appeared in the newspapers and she became a tourist attraction. title: Hogg Family History Scotland To Wanganui NZ Reunion Events + Genealogy
body:
Hogg Family History Scotland To Wanganui New Zealand Reunion Events And Family Charts

This combined family history set records the journey of the Hogg family from their origins in Scotland to their establishment in Wanganui and the generations that followed.

After arriving in Whanganui, he became the first resident Presbyterian Minister, and he and Sarah went on to have a further 13 children. David travelled up and down the coast preaching, often riding on a bullock named ‘Albert’ while writing his sermons.
Sarah, known for her kindness and hospitality, went on to establish a very successful millinery business
in Ayrshire House on Campbell Place (now Rutland Street) in Whanganui.

The contents of the second volume focus on the 135th anniversary celebrations held on 27 February 1988 and provide a structured record of the events that marked the occasion. These include the reunion dinner, memorabilia displays, a church service, a bus tour of significant sites and a graveside service honouring early family members. The book also includes entries from a family Bible that offer insight into births, marriages and deaths across generations.

This detailed history of St Andrew Kilwinning Lodge No 79 traces sixty years of Masonic activity from its beginnings in 1868 through to 1928 and provides a comprehensive record of the lodge’s development within the New Zealand Constitution. The book was written by W Bro N G Armstrong and printed in 1930 and reflects the traditions values and organisational structure of Freemasonry during a period of significant growth in Wanganui. The narrative begins with the formation of the lodge and outlines the early meetings the challenges of establishing a stable membership and the influence of the Scottish Constitution under which the lodge originally operated.  The text also highlights the contributions of prominent members whose leadership shaped the lodge’s identity and direction. The period of the Great War is given particular attention with recognition of members who served and the impact of wartime conditions on lodge activities. The inclusion of illustrations portraits and photographs of meeting places adds depth to the historical record and provides visual context for the people and events described. Lists of members and officers further enhance the value of the book for genealogical and historical research. This publication stands as an important record of Masonic heritage in Wanganui and preserves the legacy of a lodge that played a significant role in the community. Date of publishing 1930.

title: The Piraki Log Diary Of Captain Hempleman New Zealand Whaling  Published 1910
body:
The Piraki Log Diary Of Captain Hempleman New Zealand Whaling History Anson Edition Oxford 1910

The Piraki Log - A significant early record of whaling activity settlement and coastal life around Banks Peninsula. The volume includes an introduction glossary illustrations and a detailed map which together provide context for the events described in the original manuscript. The diary was edited and published by F A Anson who served as chairman of the Akaroa County Council and who ensured that the manuscript was preserved and deposited in the museum at Christchurch. The book offers insight into the daily operations of a whaling station including provisions lists gear inventories slop accounts and the names of vessels and crew members associated with the station. The contents also include records of whales caught equipment lost and stores used across several years which makes the book a valuable resource for maritime historians and researchers studying early European activity in New Zealand. The map of Banks Peninsula included in the publication highlights bays headlands and Maori place names which adds geographical depth to the diary entries.The detailed lists and chronological entries provide a rare look at the logistics of nineteenth century whaling operations and the relationships between crew members vessels and the coastal environment. This edition is of interest to collectors of New Zealand maritime history those researching Banks Peninsula and anyone seeking original source material relating to early settlement and whaling. 

Published 1910 Hardback 171 pages Uncut pages some spotting

title: Brind Of The Bay Of Islands NZ Whaling History + Early Settlement 1830s to 1850
body:
Brind Of The Bay Of Islands presents a concise account of thirty years in the life of a whaling captain working throughout the Bay of Islands in the early nineteenth century. Written by Jocelyn Chisholm and published in 1979 in New Zealand as a limited edition of four hundred copies, the book records maritime activity early settlement and the relationships that shaped the region. 

Drawn from journals letters and early reports, the narrative outlines voyages encounters and events that influenced the north during a period of change. It follows the development of whaling stations ship movements and the tensions that arose as traders missionaries and local communities interacted. Key moments such as the Girls War the land claims period and conflicts of the 1830s and 1840s are presented with context that shows their impact on the Bay of Islands. The structure is chronological beginning with introductory material before moving through three main sections covering each decade. The contents trace early voyages and family links through to later political and social developments. Final chapters include aftermath notes and references that support further research. 

This edition includes full publication details confirming its authenticity and collectible status. 

. Condition VG  92 pages

• Introduction • Acknowledgements • Part One 1820–1830 – Cumberland – Asp – Family – Toward Castle – Tonga • Part Two 1830–1840 – Girls War – Missionaries – Whaling – Narwhal • Part Three 1840–1850 – Murder – Land Claims – War In The North – Last Years • Aftermath • Notes And References

title: Prehistoric Man In Palliser Bay Wairarapa Maori Archaelogical Settlements 272pgs
body:
Published as National Museum of New Zealand Bulletin 21 (1979), Prehistoric Man in Palliser Bay presents the results of the Wairarapa Archaeological Research Programme, a major investigation into early Māori settlement along the eastern Wairarapa coast. Edited by B. Foss Leach and Helen M. Leach, the volume brings together excavation reports, environmental studies, and analyses of subsistence, horticulture, and community structure. It remains a central reference for understanding early occupation in a region that preserves some of the most informative archaeological landscapes in New Zealand.

The contents include studies of protohistoric Māori settlement patterns, the Mokau Valley sequence, marine resource exploitation at Black Rocks, and the extensive Washpool Valley excavations. Further chapters examine prehistoric gardens, stone procurement, burial orientations, sea mammal hunting, land snail assemblages, and environmental change. The concluding synthesis outlines the organisation of early communities in eastern Palliser Bay, drawing on radiocarbon sequences, faunal remains, and landscape evidence to reconstruct settlement development prior to European contact.



• National Museum of New Zealand Bulletin 21
• Edited by B. F. Leach and H. M. Leach
• Comprehensive archaeological studies of Palliser Bay
• Includes excavation reports and environmental analyses
• Key reference for early Māori settlement research

title: Prehistoric Maori Pa Sites & Fortifications In The North Island Of New Zealand
body:
This publication by Aileen Fox provides a detailed archaeological study of prehistoric Maori fortifications in the North Island and remains a significant reference for understanding the development of pa sites and defensive structures. The book draws on literary sources pictorial evidence and extensive field surveys to present a clear interpretation of how fortified settlements were constructed organised and adapted over time. The introduction outlines the aims of the study and places the work within the wider context of New Zealand archaeological research. The chapters examine the evidence and its interpretation the varieties of pa and the evolution of defensive features including ramparts ditches palisades and strategic placement on ridges and hilltops. Further sections explore internal planning such as houses storage pits cooking areas and sacred spaces which together illustrate the complexity of community life within fortified settlements. The book also discusses comparisons between Maori pa and British hill forts highlighting similarities in defensive design social organisation and the relationship between landscape and settlement. The contents include a glossary of Maori terms a bibliography and a list of illustrations which support the text and provide useful reference material for researchers.

title: Wax From Peat from The Chatham Islands Published 1944
body:
This report was published by the Department of Scientific and Industrial Research (DSIR) during World War II. At the time, global supply chains were disrupted, and New Zealand was searching for local alternatives to imported industrial materials.

Part I (Geological): Written by E.O. Macpherson, a prominent New Zealand geologist. This section focuses on the location, depth, and quantity of the peat deposits.

Part II (Analytical): Written by W.G. Hughson, focusing on the chemical properties of the wax, how it could be extracted, and its potential industrial applications.

Why "Wax from Peat"?
You might wonder why anyone would want to squeeze wax out of a swamp. During the 1940s, high-grade mineral waxes (like Montan wax, usually imported from Germany) were essential for:

Carbon paper (crucial for record-keeping before digital tech).

Polishes for boots and floors.

Lubricants and waterproofing agents for military equipment.

Phonograph records.

The Chatham Islands contain massive peat deposits, some several meters deep. The DSIR researchers discovered that this peat contained a high percentage of wax (sometimes over 10%) that was chemically similar to the expensive waxes used in Europe.

Historical Significance
While the research proved that the wax was there and of high quality, a full-scale industry never quite took off. The isolation of the Chatham Islands made transport costs prohibitively expensive once international trade stabilized after the war. However, this document remains a gold standard for NZ geological history and is often cited in modern studies regarding peatland conservation and carbon storage.

body:
Gumfields of Aupouri

Gumfields of Aupouri by Roy Wagener is a personal and historical account of life on the kauri gumfields of the Far North. Written in 1977, this booklet serves as a first-hand record of an era that shaped the economic and social landscape of Northland. The author, born in Pukenui in 1924, provides a nostalgic yet grounded perspective on the hard work and unique community that existed around the Houhora Harbour. This narrative is particularly significant for its portrayal of the gumdigging industry, which served as a primary source of income for many local residents during the early 20th century. This work is a valuable addition to any collection of New Zealand regional history or industrial heritage.

The text captures the atmosphere of the Far North gumlands as detailed in the preface and main body:
* Childhood memories of Pukenui and Houhora Harbour
* The role of the Northern Steam Ship Company in local trade
* Descriptions of kauri gum bullock wagons and early lorries
* The significance of the local storekeeper and postmaster
* Environmental impacts of gumland fires and clearing
* The transition from gumdigging to economic farm units
* Personal anecdotes of a "Huckleberry Finn" playground in the north
* Observations on the aromatic presence of ambergris and kauri gum

This Softcover booklet offers a vivid depiction of a busy city-like port atmosphere in an isolated community, where ships were hidden under piles of bagged gum. Wagener describes the manual labor of gumdigging, the environmental conditions of the peat smoke and scrub fires, and the eventual development of the land for agricultural use.  Published in 1977.

title: A History Gisborne Harbour A Tragedy By Thos Todd 1946
body:
History Gisborne Harbour A Tragedy

History of Gisborne Harbour by Thos. Todd is a remarkable and rare historical record of the maritime struggles of Poverty Bay. Written in 1939 and published in 1946, this work is subtitled "A Tragedy," documenting the story of a harbour that came within reach of success only to have its development scrapped by the Board. This specific copy is highly collectible, featuring a personal inscription from the author that reads: "With the compliments of the author." Todd, who served as a member and deputy-chairman of the Harbour Board for thirteen years, writes with first-hand authority and passion, aiming to dispel misunderstandings about the harbour's failures for the benefit of future generations.

The narrative is divided into two significant parts, as outlined in the text:
* The first part: The attempt to make the river into a harbour (1885–1918)
* The second part: The attempt to build an outer harbour for Home Boats (from 1922)
* Detailed accounts of the fight against the silting of the Turanganui River
* Analysis of the 1916 silting event that ended permanent success for the inner harbour
* Technical commentary on "Reynolds' Proposed Harbour" scheme
* The impact of floods and local committee decisions on maritime infrastructure
* Lessons learned from the Historical Committee records of Poverty Bay
* A comprehensive map of the approved Gisborne Harbour scheme

It includes a detailed map titled "Reynolds' Proposed Harbour," showing future wharves, swinging areas, and breakwater plans in relation to the Turanganui River and Kaiti Hills. Published in 1946.

title: A History Of 250 Old Ringing Bells Of New Zealand
body:
People have asked us how we got the idea to collect bell histories in New Zealand. It started in a simple manner. When we handed over our 'Brooksdale' bell to a near relative, we realized that its history would be lost unless it was recorded. Then a lady told us of the old Macetown school bell. From then on we were away and gathering momentum.

As far as possible we have wrapped a story round each bell; we have spent four years on the project and know that there are still hundreds of bells that we have never heard about. For this first volume we had to have a deadline, so 250 seemed a reasonable number. 

 Internally clean and crisp. A nice copy.

title: Foote Bros 7 Brothers & 6 Siters Kauri Timber Days 1865 - 1966 Far North  SIGNED
body:
Life & Times Of 7 Brothers & 6 Siters In The Kauri Timber Days 

Foote Prints Among The Kauri is the story of the lives and times of seven brothers and six sisters, and the kauri timber industry around them.

In the 1890's, after milling at Whananaki and Maungatapere, near Whangarei, the partnership of Foote Bros felled and milled thousands of burnt and green kauri trees from the great Puhipuhi forest.

With their energy and innovation they paved the way for the use of steam-powered traction engines and log haulers in the Northland bush and led the establishment of the Whangarei Engineering Company.

Sisters Foote were amongst New Zealand's earliest nurses, playing a leading role during the formative years of the profession and through World War I.

The family's early days in Newfoundland, emigration to New Zealand in a small brigantine, and the establishment of one of the country's first steam-powered saw mills, near the Manukau Heads.
The lives of the thirteen children of William and Elizabeth Foote are traced around Northland as they touch Whananaki and Whangaruru, Maungatapere and Whangarei, Herekino and Hokianga. Fred and Will Foote are followed across USA and Canada as they journey back to their roots in Newfoundland at the turn of the century.

The book looks back from today at changing attitudes to the exploitation of the kauri forests and guides the reader to the sites of some early mills and the signs from the past.

Bill Haigh, a civil engineer living in Kerikeri, is a great-grandson of one of the seven Foote brothers.

title: First Fifty Years Marton Sash And Door Timber Co Ltd Rangitikei 1957 R A Wilson
body:
This industrial history documents the first half-century of the Marton Sash and Door Timber Co. Ltd., providing a detailed look at the timber industry in the Rangitīkei and central North Island regions. Written by R. A. Wilson, the record moves beyond simple corporate history to provide technical insights into early 20th-century sawmilling and bush work.

A significant portion of the text is dedicated to the working of the Rangataua Mill, specifically detailing the reliance on bullock-power. It records the transition from traditional animal-drawn transport to the introduction of log haulers and tractors. The narrative includes practical observations on bush craft, from the construction of tramlines using O.B. matai wooden rails to the management and feeding of the bullock teams that were essential to the mill's output.

Factual details and documented history include:
* Detailed records of the Rangataua Mill operations, including contract prices for log measure.

title: An illustrated history of the Mount Rochfort Railway and the Denniston Incline
body:
Coaling from the clouds
 R.J. Meyer

An illustrated brief history of the Mount Rochfort railway and the Denniston Incline


ONE OF WESTLAND’S MOST dramatic engineering marvels, the Denniston Incline, is the main feature of Westport’s coalmining museum, Coaltown. In 1859, surveyor John Rochfort first discovered coal in a small creek 14km north of Westport. However, it was not until 1860 that Dr Julius von Haast found seams of prized bituminous coal high on the fog-shrouded plateau which bears Rochfort’s name.

Before it could be used to power the industrial machinery of last century, the coal had to be transported down from the plateau. The ingenious construction of the Dennis-ton Incline in 1878-79 overcame the problem by using the coal’s very height above sea level to shift it to the railhead.

64 pages cardcovered with index

1971

title: The History Of  TheTaranaki  Province New Zealand NZ 1878 Edition
body:
The History of Taranaki is a standard work on the history of the province first published in 1878. The text provides a detailed study of the region from both Maori and European viewpoints. Content covers the essence of Taranaki history including land disputes, unscrupulous dealings, and judgements by the Governor. Narrative accounts include pioneer life and the arrival of the first horse which was called Kuri nui by the Maori audience. 
Chapters document Maori traditions respecting the Morioris and the resemblance of customs to tribes in India, China, and ancient Africa. An Indian ship bell found in New Zealand is mentioned alongside resemblances between Maori and Egyptian words. Historical records detail the naming of Taranaki rivers and hills, the arrival of the Ngatiawa at the Bay of Islands, and the subduing of aborigines at Ngapuketurua. Documentation includes the canoes of Maori immigrants and the location of various hapus. Military history includes the assault on Pukerangiora, the massacre of the garrison, and the defeat of the Waikatos and much more

title: From Gold To Green Paeroa And District 1875 1975 NZ History Goldfields Centenary
body:
From Gold To Green Paeroa and District 1875 1975 provides a historical account of the Ohinemuri area over a one hundred year period. The text documents the transition of the region from its origins as a goldfield to its development into an agricultural and transport centre. Historical records begin with the pre-European era and the inhabitants of the land prior to 1875. A significant event recorded is the signing of the Deed of Cession in 1875 by the Paramount Chiefs of Ngati Tamatera. This agreement allowed for the opening of the goldfields in an area extending through Karangahake and Waihi to the East Coast of the Coromandel Peninsula.

The emergence of the Paeroa township and its continuous growth through the century. Goldmining is documented as a primary force that shaped the local landscape. As the region developed, Paeroa became a central hub for transport. The history also examines the local river systems, documenting how they were affected by human activity and subsequent environmental changes where the rivers hit back. Administrative bodies and their roles throughout the century are detailed alongside a chronology of major events.

title: The Rangitaiki 1890 - 1990 Bay Of Plenty Tarawera Rivers Drainage System 100Yrs
body:
The Rangitaiki 1890-1990 documents the extensive history of the Rangitaiki swamp, The text covers a century of environmental transformation, beginning with the initial 1890 survey when the region was a roadless wilderness of flax, raupo, and manuka. Surveyors and prospective settlers believed the land would be fertile if drained, leading to the establishment of the first Drainage Board and the offering of 500-acre blocks for selection. Documentation details the impact of major natural events, including a mammoth deluge in July 1892 that turned the area into an inland lake and the earthquake of 2 March 1987 which caused subterranean subsidence.

The narrative follows the technical and political challenges of dewatering the swamp, including the unsuccessful attempts by the second Drainage Board and the eventual restoration of settler control through the Rangitaiki Land Drainage Act in 1956. Detailed records examine the construction of the public drainage system from 1911 to 1925, covering both eastern and western areas. The history expands into the development of regional infrastructure, documenting the arrival of roads, bridges, and the railway. Final sections address the growth of farming, the aftermath of the 1925 Commission.

title: A Short History Of The Church Of St Stephen The Martyr Opotiki 1864 Records NZ
body:
A Short History of the Church of St Stephen the Martyr Opotiki New Zealand provides a chronological record of the Anglican parish in the Bay of Plenty. The text documents the establishment of the mission and the subsequent construction of the church building. It contains factual accounts of the events of 1864 including the death of Reverend Carl Sylvius Volkner. The publication details the transformation of the church during the New Zealand Wars when it was used as a military redoubt.

Early Mission Foundation: Documents the arrival of the Church Missionary Society in the Opotiki district and the initial interactions with the local population.

1864 Conflict: Provides a factual timeline of the events leading to the death of the resident missionary and the impact on the church structure.

Military Occupation: Records the period when the church was fortified and used by the government forces during the campaign in the eastern Bay of Plenty.

Reconstruction and Consecration: Details the physical repairs made to the building and its return to use as a place of worship for the Opotiki community.

Internal Memorials: Lists the various plaques and memorials situated within the church that record the names of individuals associated with its history.

Parish Governance: Traces the list of vicars and lay leaders who managed the ecclesiastical affairs of the church through the nineteenth and twentieth centuries.

The listing includes information regarding the architectural features of the building and the specific dates of its various additions. It serves as a record of the social and religious evolution of Opotiki and the wider regional heritage. The text is based on archival research and primary parish documents.

body:

Craig and published in January 1985 as Monograph Number 12 by the Whakatane and District Historical Society. This first edition was limited to 300 copies. The volume documents Gudgeon's career as a soldier, settler, judge of the Native Land Court, Under Secretary for Defence, Commissioner of Police, and Resident Commissioner of the Cook Islands.

The text records his transition to the judicial bench of the Native Land Court and his decade-long administration of the Cook Islands, including the annexation period and the regional trade disputes referred to as the Battle of the Bananas. title: Captain Cadell & The Waikato Flotilla - River Pioneer History New Zealand & Aus
body:
Captain Cadell and the Waikato Flotilla was written by Robert D. Campbell and documents the maritime and military career of Captain Francis Cadell. The text focuses on his role as a pioneer of navigation on the Murray River and his subsequent service as the Superintendent of Steam Transport for the New Zealand Government between 1864 and 1866.

The narrative provides a factual record of the steam vessels used during the New Zealand wars including technical details and operational histories of the P.S. Pioneer P.S. Koheroa and the P.S. Sturt. It documents Cadell’s inspection of the P.S. Luna a former blockade runner and the logistical challenges of ascending the Wanganui River rapids. The book includes a detailed list of illustrations from sources such as the Alexander Turnbull Library and the State Library of South Australia. Factual accounts of Cadell's later explorations in Northern Australia and the Pacific are also included providing a comprehensive record of nineteenth-century steam transport and colonial maritime history. The text details the construction of the steam barge White Slave and the preservation of the P.S. Rangiriri as a historic monument in Hamilton. Further documentation includes the use of useful steamers in the coastal service for conveying dispatches and stores. Appendices and photographic sources are indexed for research.

* Twentieth Century Development: Kakariki and Willow Flat 1900-50
* Land Tenure: Twentieth-century ownership changes 1900-50
* Forestry Transition: Conversion to Exotic Forest

The report details how the region was typical of many areas in Hawkes Bay where the land was only marginally suitable for farming. It records the specific techniques used by nineteenth-century farmers and the reliance on pack tracks for the transport of goods and stock. The text provides a record of the impact of the Mohaka massacre on local settlement and the subsequent shifts in land ownership. Detailed sections cover the management of stations at Kakariki and Willow Flat during the first half of the twentieth century. The publication concludes with the final conversion of these farming blocks into the Mohaka State Forest. This document was originally produced in Napier and contains research intended for the Conservator of Forests.

title: Captain Hannibal Marks 1819-1879 Gunboat Commander & Tauranga Harbour Master NZ
body:
This biographical record documents the life and maritime career of Captain Hannibal Marks (1819-1879). Born in Cawsand, Cornwall, Marks arrived in New Zealand in 1841 following the wreck of the schooner Regina at New Plymouth. The text provides a factual account of his progression from a coastal trader on the west coast of the North Island to a significant figure in New Zealand's colonial naval history.

* Early Arrival: Details the 1841 wreck of the Regina and Marks' initial years in the Taranaki settlement.
* Manukau Harbour Service: Records his tenure as the first pilot of Manukau Harbour in 1853 and his work as mate on the government cutter Maori.
* Gunboat Command: Documents his service as commander of the H.M. Colonial gunboats Caroline (formerly the Ruby) and the Sandfly during the New Zealand Wars.
* War Service 1861-1865: Traces his role in delivering despatches for Governor Grey and enforcing naval blockades in the Firth of Thames and Tauranga.
* Tauranga Harbour Master: Records his appointment as Harbour Master and Pilot at Tauranga in 1873 following the re-establishment of the position.
* 1879 Drowning Incident: Factual account of the August 1879 tragedy in Tauranga Harbour where Marks and his son, also named Hannibal, drowned while attempting to assist racing yachts in foul weather.

The record details his professional interactions with historical figures such as Sir George Grey and Captain Francis Cadell. It provides technical data regarding the vessels he commanded, including the 25-ton schooner Caroline and the steamer Sandfly. The text also notes his involvement in maritime inquiries and his burial at the Mission Cemetery in Tauranga

title: Piriaka The Story Of A Main Trunk Settlement 1901-1981 Railway & Sawmill History
body:
Piriaka the story of a Main Trunk settlement 1901-1981 is a commemorative souvenir booklet produced for the Piriaka School 70th Jubilee. The text provides a factual chronological history of the settlement located in the King Country region of New Zealand. It documents the industrial and social development of the district from the opening of the King Country in the early 1900s through to the jubilee celebrations in 1981.

* Early Settlement: Records the arrival of the railway and its role in the establishment of the town including the early town plan and postal services.
* Industrial Infrastructure: Provides factual data regarding the commencement of bushfelling in the 1900s sawmill sites and the development of the local dairy factory which served the area for 64 years.
* Local Utilities: Documents the construction of the power station in 1923 and the establishment of the regional abattoir.
* Educational History: Traces the history of Piriaka School from 1903 to 1907 and its subsequent growth including the teacher roll call and former pupils.
* Social & Community Life: Records the activities of the Maori Women’s Welfare League sport and social life and the decade-by-decade groups of the district.
* Jubilee Commemoration: Detailed accounts of the 70th Jubilee celebrations including the committee and the status of former residents in 1981.

It utilizes official records and personal interviews with former residents to provide a factual account of the community's evolution. It further documents the development of the swimming baths and aviary as part of the town’s social infrastructure. This booklet is a central reference for genealogical and regional research into the Piriaka and Manunui districts.

title: Putaruru Local History Small Beginnings Bold Measures From Earlest Times -1990's
body:
Putaruru: Home of the Owl provides a factual and comprehensive chronological account of the development of the Putaruru district from the seventeenth century through to the late twentieth century. This volume serves as a primary reference for the social, cultural, and economic transformation of the region, documenting the lives of the people and the growth of the community.

* Tangata Whenua and Early Settlement: Records the history of the Tangata Whenua from 1600 to 1880, documenting the traditional land use and the initial transition toward European contact.
* Small Beginnings to the Roaring Twenties: Factual documentation of the district’s establishment between 1880 and 1919, followed by the rapid expansion and social changes characterized by the decade of the 1920s.
* Bold Measures and Hard Times: Traces the community’s resilience through the 1930s, documenting the impact of the economic depression and the specific local measures taken to ensure regional stability.
* The War Years and Aftermath: Records the significant changes brought about by the Second World War between 1940 and 1947, followed by the dawn of a new age of development and infrastructure through 1953.
* The Baby Boom and Growth: Documents the mid-century building boom from 1953 to 1960 and the subsequent social atmosphere of the 1960s, often remembered as the good old days of regional prosperity.
* Modern Planning and Adaptation: Traces the legislative and community planning efforts of the 1970s, the increased pace of development in the 1980s, and the strategic changes required for survival in a modern economic landscape.
*  comprehensive appendices, local government records, and an extensive bibliography, making it an essential tool for genealogical and regional research.

title: A Biography of William White 1794-1875 Wesleyan Missionary at Whangaroa Hokianga
body:
Mana at Mangungu provides a factual and chronological account of one of the most significant historical sites in the Hokianga district. This volume documents the establishment of the Wesleyan Mission at Mangungu, Horeke, and its pivotal role in the early interactions between Māori and European settlers, including the regional signing of the Treaty of Waitangi.

* Mission Establishment: Records the 1828 founding of the Mangungu Mission Station, documenting the logistical challenges of the Hokianga landscape and the early Wesleyan influence in the North.
* Māori and European Contact: Factual documentation of the evolving relationship between the Wesleyan missionaries and local iwi, including the social and religious impact of the mission on the Hokianga community.
* Te Tiriti o Waitangi: Detailed history of the February 1840 signing of the Treaty of Waitangi at Mangungu, recording the largest single signing event and the involvement of local rangatira.
* The Mission House: Traces the history of the Mangungu Mission House, including its construction, its eventual removal to Onehunga, and its return to the original Horeke site as a heritage landmark.
* Regional Development: Records the role of the mission in the growth of the Horeke township and its significance as a center for trade, communication, and early colonial administration.
* Primary Source Documentation: Utilizing archival mission records, Māori oral traditions, and government documents to provide an accurate record of the site’s enduring mana and national significance.
