title: The Redoubts Of The Upper Waikato Military History And Fortifications Records
body:
The Redoubts of the Upper Waikato provides a factual and chronological account of the military fortifications established during the nineteenth-century Waikato War. This volume documents the strategic placement and construction of various redoubts, recording the military and social history of the frontier settlements.

* Military Strategy and Construction: Records the planning and engineering of redoubts across the Upper Waikato, documenting the defensive measures taken during the conflict and the logistical role of the military forces.
* Regional Fortifications: Detailed documentation of specific sites including the Alexandra Redoubt, Kihikihi, and the Te Awamutu military posts, recording the layout and garrison history of each position.
* The Frontier Conflict: Factual accounts of the engagements at Orakau and the surrounding districts, documenting the impact of the war on both military personnel and the developing settler community.
* Settlement Development: Traces the transition of these military outposts into established townships such as Cambridge, Ohaupo, and Te Awamutu, recording the foundational infrastructure that remained after the conflict.
* Primary Source Documentation: Utilizing archival military maps, contemporary sketches, and official records to provide an accurate account of the defensive lines and the soldiers who manned them.

This publication serves as a primary reference for the military, social, and genealogical history of the Waikato region. It provides specific data regarding the locations of lost redoubts and the pioneering families who settled the frontier.

title: History of the Martinborough District From Earlest Times 334 Pages
body:
The Canoes of Kupe is a comprehensive account of the history of the Martinborough district from the beginnings of settlement by Maori through to the booming present-day wine industry.

This book is based on extensive research in private and public archives, as well as in the secondary literature, and also makes use of oral history interviews.

 Primarily a narrative history which makes excellent use of illustrative individuals and families, industries and events, it is also of wider than local significance, setting the story of Martinborough within national, and at times international, social and economic contexts.

Beautifully designed and copiously illustrated, this book is a treasure for residents and visitors alike.

title: A Short History Of The Church Of St Stephen The Martyr Opotiki 1864 Records NZ
body:
A Short History of the Church of St Stephen the Martyr Opotiki New Zealand provides a chronological record of the Anglican parish in the Bay of Plenty. The text documents the establishment of the mission and the subsequent construction of the church building. It contains factual accounts of the events of 1864 including the death of Reverend Carl Sylvius Volkner. The publication details the transformation of the church during the New Zealand Wars when it was used as a military redoubt.

Early Mission Foundation: Documents the arrival of the Church Missionary Society in the Opotiki district and the initial interactions with the local population.

1864 Conflict: Provides a factual timeline of the events leading to the death of the resident missionary and the impact on the church structure.

Military Occupation: Records the period when the church was fortified and used by the government forces during the campaign in the eastern Bay of Plenty.

Reconstruction and Consecration: Details the physical repairs made to the building and its return to use as a place of worship for the Opotiki community.

Internal Memorials: Lists the various plaques and memorials situated within the church that record the names of individuals associated with its history.

Parish Governance: Traces the list of vicars and lay leaders who managed the ecclesiastical affairs of the church through the nineteenth and twentieth centuries.

The listing includes information regarding the architectural features of the building and the specific dates of its various additions. It serves as a record of the social and religious evolution of Opotiki and the wider regional heritage. The text is based on archival research and primary parish documents.

body:
Historical records covering the period 1857 to 1982 document the establishment and development of St Matthew the Apostle Church at Tuparoa Bay. The text identifies early religious influences including the first herald of Christianity in the Waiapu and detailed diary references to Tuparoa spanning 1844, 1846, 1847, 1848, 1849, 1850, 1854, 1855, 1856, 1857, and 1861. title: History Of And Guide To The Wanganui River 2nd Edition Local NZ History Guide
body:
This second edition publication provides a detailed historical account and geographical guide to the Wanganui River region. The text covers the early navigation of the river, bird life, and the various species of forest trees found in the area. It documents the journey downstream and provides specific details on the Houseboat Marae-kowhai. Historical events and locations included in the record are the Battle of Moutoa, the Sale of Whanganui in 1840, and the establishment of the Wanganui town and suburbs. Factual sketches and descriptions include Māori types, the Old Cliff Ladder at Arawhata, and the Drop Scene at Aratira. Geographical landmarks and settlements documented include Taumarunui, Retaruke, Kirikiriroa, Puketapu, Manganui-o-te-ao, Pipiriki, Atene, and Hiruharama.

Contents:
Preface, Taumarunui, Early River Navigation, Bird Life on the River, Forest Trees, Journey Down Stream, The Houseboat, Retaruke, Kirikiriroa, Puketapu, Manganui-o-te-ao, Pipiriki, Battle of Moutoa, Atene, Kemp's Pole, Tamati, The Sale of Whanganui, Maori Pronunciation, Wanganui Town and Suburbs.

Illustrations:
Wanganui from Lift Tower Durie Hill, Maori Types, The Houseboat, Retaruke, Old Cliff Ladder, The Drop Scene, Pipiriki House, Hiruharama, Kaiwhaike and Tamati 1890, Sale of Whanganui 1840.

Maps:
The volume contains eight section maps located on pages 14, 17, 32, 39, 47, 57, 66, and 72. 

The text provides a primary source account of the region, detailing the river's historical significance and the progression of settlement along its banks.

title: Archaeological Excavations Omata Stockade Warea Redoubt NZ Wars History Taranaki
body:
This monograph from the New Zealand Archaeological Association provides a formal record of excavations conducted at two significant military sites in the Taranaki region. The research focuses on the Omata Stockade and the Warea Redoubt, offering a detailed analysis of the fortifications and the living conditions during the New Zealand Wars. The text includes an introduction to the conflict within Taranaki and the specific role of these defensive structures. Technical data is provided regarding the history of the Omata Stockade, the excavation process, and a comprehensive study of recovered artefacts. Similarly, the work documents the surface evidence, excavation findings, and historical context of the Warea Redoubt.


Introduction, The New Zealand Wars, Taranaki, The Fortifications, The Research, Archaeological Excavations At The Omata Stockade, History Of The Omata Stockade, The Excavation, The Artefacts, Conclusions, Archaeological Excavations At Warea Redoubt, History Of Warea Redoubt, Surface Evidence, The Excavation, The Artefacts, The Means And Ends Of Colonial Warfare, The Defences, Accommodation, Artefacts, The Industrial Revolution And Colonial Economy, Bibliography.

The documentation serves as a critical archaeological and historical resource, utilizing physical evidence to reconstruct the daily realities of military life in 19th-century New Zealand. It provides precise details on the construction of earthworks and the material culture of the period through the analysis of site-specific findings. This volume is part of the New Zealand Archaeological Association Monograph series and represents a major contribution to the recorded history of Taranaki's military landscape.

title: Elsdon Best The The Whare Kohanga And Its Lore
body:
Elsdon Best The The Whare Kohanga And Its Lore

1929. The Whare Kohanga (the "Nest House") and its lore: Comprising data pertaining to procreation, baptism, and infant betrothal, etc.: Contirbuted by Members of the Ngati-Kahungunu Tribe of the North Island of New Zealand. Wellington
Elsdon Best was a prolific recorder of pre-European Maori social life and material culture. His work established him as New Zealand's foremost ethnographer of Maori society.

Best based is research on rare first-hand knowledge, wide reading, informed study and discussion with Maori. His was a lifelong project underpinned by a belief that Maori were a people under threat of extinction, yet by the time of his death in 1931 Best's work had assumed new importance as it informed a Maori cultural renaissance.

Today, the Elsdon Best books are accepted as classic studies. They remain a uniquely valuable record of traditional Maori culture, social customs and beliefs.

title: From The Beginning The Archaeology Of The Maori Pre-European NZ Archaeology
body:
This volume serves as a comprehensive introduction to the systematic archaeological investigations of Māori history and pre-European life in NZ. Produced in association with the NZ Historic Places Trust, the text outlines findings regarding the origins, settlement patterns, and cultural evolution of the earliest Polynesian arrivals. Detailed chapters examine the physical and health characteristics of early populations alongside the manufacturing processes for stone and wood implements. The research covers food sources from forest and coast, gardening techniques, foraging, and the development of dwellings and fortifications. Factual accounts describe personal decoration, garments, domestic utensils, and the artistic beauty of taonga. The book includes a Māori perspective on archaeological work, addressing cultural beliefs and the ownership of the past. Technical content is supported by numerous photographs of archaeological finds, colour reconstructions of ancient lifestyles, drawings, diagrams, and maps. The text documents the transition from early settlement to the flowering of Classic Māori culture and the impact of these populations on the NZ landscape. Appendices provide information on the protection and management of archaeological sites.

title: Colonial Architecture In Early NZ 1840s To 1880s Regional Building History Book
body:
This architectural history provides a detailed survey of building styles and construction in NZ from the early nineteenth century through to the 1880s. The text documents the progression from the earliest forerunners and the settlements of the 1840s to the established regional styles of later decades. Specific chapters focus on the development of Otago and Canterbury, mid-century influences, and the emergence of the Selwyn style under architects Mountfort and Thatcher. The research examines the thriving building periods of the 1860s across both the North and South Islands, including dedicated sections on architecture for government use and the specific evolution of Auckland, Wellington, and Southland during the 1870s and 1880s. 

The volume features an extensive collection of historical photographs, including views of Nelson with Mountfort extensions, alongside drawings and architectural plans.

The work is a primary reference for the study of the NZ built environment and the transition from temporary shelters to permanent stone and timber structures.

title: The New Zealand Division on the Western Front 1916 - 1918  - 408 pages
body:
The New Zealand Division's campaign in France and Flanders was the birth of the modern New Zealand Army. At Armentieres, First Somme 1916, Messines and Third Ypres 1917 it established a high reputation. 

This was enhanced in its great encounter battle when it closed a gap in the British Line on the Ancre in Second Somme, during the German Spring Offensive in March 1918. Following this, it was in the vanguard of the final Advance to Victory in the Last Hundred Days, culminating in its heroic, medieval Relief of Le Quesnoy in the Battle of the Sambre, at war's end.
 This story tells of national sacrifice. 13,647 New Zealanders died within the Division's ranks in two and a half years; more than in all three Armed Services combined in the six years of World War II. The book demonstrates that casualties were consistently under-reported in Stewart. The campaign in France and Belgian Flanders has long lingered in the shadow of Gallipoli's legendary status. The author lightens that shadow in arguing convincingly that the Western Front was much more significant militarily and historically, and deserves at least equal prominence in New Zealand's national consciousness. This was one of his aims in writing the book. The Western Front campaign was the only occasion in New Zealand's military history when its Division contributed to the defeat of the main army of the main enemy, in the main theatre of war. The foreword is by Dr Christopher Pugsley, New Zealand's pre-eminent authority on the Great War.

title: Matters of the Heart A History of Interracial Marriage in New Zealand
body:
Matters of the Heart  A History of Interracial Marriage in New Zealand

From whalers and traders marrying into Maori families in the early 19th century to the growth of interracial marriages in the later 20th, Matters of the Heart unravels the long history of interracial relationships in New Zealand. It encompasses common law marriages and Maori customary marriages, alongside formal arrangements recognized by church and state, and shows how public policy and private life were woven together. It also explores the gamut of official reactions—from condemnation of interracial immorality or racial treason to celebration of New Zealand’s unique intermarriage patterns as a sign of its progressive attitude toward race relations. This social history focuses on the lives and experiences of real Maori and Pakeha people and reveals New Zealand’s changing attitudes to race, marriage, and intimacy.

title: True Story Of The Breaker Morant's Bushveldt Carbineers - Boer War  AUSTRALIA
body:
During the Boer War in South Africa, Australian George Ramsdale Witton (1874 -1942) served as a lieutenant in the Bushveldt Carbineers until he was sentenced to death for murder after the shooting of nine Boer prisoners.

Witton's scathing political indictment of the British Empire during the Boer War, originally published in 1907. The basis for the movie "Breaker Morant". Witton's main assertion, as indicated by the book's provocative title, was that he, Morant, and Handcock were made scapegoats by the British authorities in South Africa - that they were made to take the blame for widespread British war crimes against the Boers, and that the trial and executions were carried out by the British for political reasons, partly to cover up a controversial and secret "no prisoners" policy by Kitchener, and partly to appease the Boer government over the killing of Boer prisoners, in order to facilitate a peace treaty; the Treaty of Vereeniging was signed on 31 May 1902.

title: 3rd New Zealand Division in the South Pacific World War II  Pacific Star  VG
body:
Pacific Star 3 NZ Division in the South Pacific in World War I Hardback

The war in the South Pacific saw some of the most gruelling fighting of World War Two. Conditions were unpleasant - fetid heat, torrential downpours and hostile flora and fauna - while the Japanese enemy were ruthless and would often fight to the last man. Amphibious warfare, jungle warfare and the need to co-operate with our American ally all meant that new demands were made on New Zealand soldiers. 

Yet the war in the South Pacific came to be seen as an easy war, generally overlooked (with the exception of Guadalcanal) by historians. From ignorance of what the soldiers were called upon to endure, the notion arose that service in the South Pacific was somehow less worthy than the 'real war' in Europe against the Nazis. 

This attitude continued in the post-war world and today the soldiers of 3NZ Division are all but forgotten. Pacific Star sets the record straight, shedding new light on the sacrifices and tribulations which the soldiers of 3NZ Division had to endure in the service of the Allied cause. 

The book covers their initial deployment in Fiji and New Caledonia, and describes the major battles the division fought in the Solomons: Vella Lavella, the Treasury Islands and the Green Islands, as well as their sometimes strained relationship with the US military. Reg Newell has interviewed veterans for this book, which includes new maps and previously unpublished photographs.

title: Memories of Tirau A History of Tirau and District NZ South Waikato 1880 - 2000
body:
This comprehensive local history documents the development of Tirau and its surrounding districts from the late nineteenth century through to the millennium. Originally known as Oxford, the township was renamed in 1895 to reflect the prominent local peak. The narrative begins with the geological formation of the South Waikato and the early history of Ngati Raukawa, whose origins are Tainui. Significant emphasis is placed on the transition of the landscape from native bush to productive agricultural land following European settlement in the 1880s.

A significant portion of the book is dedicated to individual family stories and the recollections of long-standing pioneer settlers. These narratives offer specific details on farming life, early business enterprises, and the social fabric of the district. The final sections document the modern transformation of the town in the late 1980s and 1990s, highlighting the reinvention of the service centre into a notable tourism and antique destination. Searchable topics and sections within the work include:

title: The Wilson Family NGAIO STATION  The Purchase Of Ngaio -  Private Publication
body:
By Cynthia Wierzbicka

This family history documents the centenary of the Wilson family's arrival and settlement at Ngaio Station in the Manawatu district. The narrative begins with the departure of James Glenny Wilson from Hawick, Scotland, and his subsequent establishment of the station near Sanson in 1873. 
The work is compiled from primary sources including personal correspondence, station diaries, and family archives. It details the operational history of Ngaio Station, including land acquisitions, stock breeding programs, and the construction of the homestead and outbuildings. Biographical sections cover multiple generations of the Wilson family, documenting their education, marriages, and contributions to the Manawatu community. The record also addresses the social life of the station, the employment of station hands, and the family's involvement with the Farmers' Union and local government.

Factual data and searchable contents within the volume include:

title: New Zealand Stretcher Bearers In The First and Second World Wars 208 pages
body:
Khaki Angles Kiwi Stretcher Bearers In The First and Second World War

Flags flying, bands playing, wonderful camaraderie and the anticipation of great adventure in places most would only have read or dreamt about were the anticipated attractions for many young men at the outset of the world wars, but the long grim reality was ugly, brutal and obscene. “Anyone who has ever looked into the glazed eyes of a soldier dying on the battlefield will think hard before starting a war.”


This book is about first-aid trained stretcher bearers, the unsung heroes attached to the field ambulances who went into the action carrying only a stretcher, a bag of field dressings, scissors and simple
medicines, and who saw equally to the needs of both friend and foe.
With text, photographs, cartoons, diagrams and copies of telegrams, letters and documents, this book is a remarkable account of the horrors of war, while inspiring respect and admiration for the bearers who were at even greater risk of becoming casualties themselves.

The illustrations are outstanding and an initial review of these encouraged a detailed read of the well written text with its information about the weapons and wounds of the Great War and World War II, frontline conditions, individual campaigns and battles, and diaries and letters home revealing the soldiers’ disillusionment with wars and
their organisation.

Khaki Angels will enlighten a wide range of readers, and for war historians

 Brendan O’Carroll has preserved an important record.

title: K Force in Korea A Soldiers Life 16th NZ Field Regiment Wilfred Poulton SIGNED
body:
This military memoir provides a first-hand account of service within the New Zealand K Force during the Korean War. The narrative follows the experiences of Wilfred Poulton, focusing on his time with the 16th New Zealand Field Regiment. The text documents the initial recruitment and training phases at Waiouru before deployment to the Korean Peninsula as part of the United Nations effort. It provides a detailed look at the operational life of a soldier within a field artillery unit, detailing the logistics, movements, and combat engagements encountered between 1950 and 1953.

The narrative tracks the regiment's participation in major actions, including the Battle of Kapyong and the defensive operations along the 38th Parallel. Detailed descriptions are provided regarding the maintenance and firing of the 25-pounder guns, the establishment of forward observation posts, and the coordination with other units within the 1st Commonwealth Division. The text also covers the non-combat aspects of service, such as the climate extremes of the Korean winter, interactions with local civilians, and the routines of camp life behind the front lines.



The work serves as a primary source for the New Zealand military contribution to the Korean conflict, preserving the personal history of a gunner's life and the tactical role of the New Zealand artillery in the campaign.

During the early years of the last century professional White Hunters were at their most successful. Safaris of 200 porters and more conveyed wealthy and aristocratic sportsmen in pursuit of game.

title: Myth & Reality The NZ Soldier in World War II John McLeod  Military History
body:
Preface

This analytical military and social history by John McLeod, an Army Officer, challenges the traditional "myth" of the New Zealand soldier as a naturally superior, outdoor-bred fighter driven by an inherent sense of decency. Drawing on extensive eyewitness accounts from ex-servicemen, the text presents a more complex and realistic appraisal of the 2NZEF during World War II. It documents the evolution of the force from enlistment and initial combat through to the mental toll of sustained warfare and the social gulf that often existed between officers and the ranks.

McLeod provides a frank examination of controversial topics within the New Zealand armed forces, including documented instances of looting, drunkenness, and panic under fire. A significant portion of the work is dedicated to the furlough drafts and the ensuing mutiny or "justifiable dissent" of soldiers who refused to return to the front. The narrative also analyzes the leadership style of General Freyberg and the realities of soldiering as a way of life. By dispelling popular misconceptions, the author aims to provide a compelling insight into the true nature of New Zealand's military culture and the human experience of the Second World War.

title: History Compulsory Military Training & National Service in New Zealand 1949 - 72
body:
Fit to Fight Compulsory Military Training and National Service in New Zealand 1949-72, By PDF Cooke.

Compulsory Military training and National Service in NZ 1949 to 1972.  

What do Colin Meads and Carmen have in common? Or Barry Brickell, Jim Bolger, John Hawkesby, Paul Reeves and Witi Ihimaera? They all took part in compulsory military training (CMT)

Just four years after the greatest war the planet had witnessed, New Zealand thought it was going to have to do it all again. As the Cold War brewed over ideology and atom bombs, New Zealand determined to play its part in collective security. 

People argued over how to raise the necessary force but the country willingly adopted Compulsory Military Training. Young men were registered, examined and forced to learn basic Army, Navy or Air Force skills. In this ground-- breaking study, military historian Peter Cooke follows these men through the process of being given a number, called up and regimented. 

The voices of over 830 trainees are heard as they fill out Labour Department forms, try for a postponement and shuffle into barracks. We witness them getting shouted at, broken -- and begrudging the discipline, discomfort and NCOs. 

But then over three months we see them emerging as confident, disciplined cogs in a machine. In almost a quarter century, around 100,000 young New Zealand men were trained in CMT and National Service. 

This was felt to be essential at the time and, thankfully, the men were never sent to war. Instead, they came out of it with something unexpected, something they've harbored for life - and were keen to reveal.

title: Going Abroad Some History Of Early Dunedin and Southland Scottish Pioneers
body:
Scotland
The Voyage
Dunedin
Southland
Appendices
Bibliography
Index

This genealogical and regional history documents the migration of the MacGibbon family and other early Scottish emigrants to the lower South Island of NZ. The text tracks the movement of these pioneers from their origins in Scotland through the arduous sea voyage to arrival in Dunedin in the mid-19th century. It provides a factual record of early settlement patterns, focusing on the establishment of families within the Otago and Southland districts.

Detailed accounts within the volume describe the transition from urban Dunedin to the rural frontier of Southland, including the first European inhabitants of areas such as Gore and the Hokonuis. The author includes specific historical anecdotes, such as the experiences of Thomas MacGibbon at Otapiri Station in 1858 and the social interactions with local figures like 'Jacky-Jacky'. This work captures the logistical realities of colonial life, from camping under drays and crossing the Mataura River to the establishment of early commercial ventures and the "Going Abroad" sale of grocery goods in 1849.

The volume serves as a primary resource for those researching Scottish ancestry in the southern regions of NZ. It includes comprehensive appendices, a bibliography for further study, and an index of names and locations relevant to the Otago and Southland pioneering era.

This significant historical work provides an account of the thousands of boys across Australia and NZ who lied about their age to enlist during the First World War of 1914–1918. Many of these young soldiers forged parental signatures to join the front lines on the other side of the world, with some as young as thirteen years old. This volume documents their experiences with clinical accuracy, moving from the initial enlistment deception to the brutal realities of the battlefield. The text focuses on the specific military engagements in Turkey during 1915 and the subsequent move to France in 1916. It includes detailed biographical records of individuals such as William Jackson, VC, and the Flemming brothers, alongside many others who served in Gallipoli and the Western Front. The research highlights the social and military pressures of the 1910s that led to such widespread under-age enlistment and the lasting impact on NZ and Australian military history. Each section is supported by maps and introductory context to place these personal accounts within the broader strategic framework of the Great War. This resource is essential for those researching ANZAC history, genealogy, or the specific personnel involved in the First World War.

title: One Flag One Queen One Tongue South African Boer War NZ Contingents & History
body:
Introduction
The Origins of New Zealand’s South African War Contribution
Desperately Seeking Service: The Australasian Commandants and the War
Opposition to the War in New Zealand
New Zealand Women’s Opposition to the South African War
The Contribution of the Colonial Forces
The Best Mounted Troops in South Africa?
The Social and Geographical Composition of the New Zealand Contingents
Different Race, Same Queen Maori and the War
New Zealand Women and the War
The Australian Perspective on the War

This provides a comprehensive examination of the NZ experience during the South African War, often referred to as the Boer War. As the first overseas conflict for NZ, this engagement involved more than 40,000 volunteers and a contribution of over 6,000 men who served primarily as mounted infantry. The text offers a detailed factual analysis of the motivations behind the first contingent’s departure from Wellington in 1899 and the subsequent raising of further units from all classes of society. It includes critical research into the social and geographical makeup of the contingents, the role of Maori personnel, and the involvement of women as nurses and schoolteachers. The essays also address the significant domestic opposition to the war from pacifists and those questioning the justice of the British cause. By exploring the wider imperial context of the British Empire, the work sheds light on how NZ's participation served as a precursor to the larger global conflicts of the twentieth century. Specific chapters focus on the commandants, the colonial forces' performance on the veldt, and the unique perspectives of women both in support of and in opposition to the struggle.

This comprehensive volume documents the centenary history of the Returned Services Association (RSA) in NZ, spanning from its foundations in 1916 through to 2016. The text provides a factual and detailed examination of the organization's role in supporting veterans returning from major global conflicts, beginning with the aftermath of the First World War and the specific challenges faced between 1916 and 1922. It explores the association's evolution through the shadow of the interwar years, the mobilization of another generation during the 1939–1945 conflict, and the "Golden Weather" of the post-WWII era. The research highlights the social and political impact of the RSA on NZ society, including its advocacy for veteran welfare, medical support, and the establishment of lasting national milestones. Specific sections detail the internal management of the association, listing patrons, presidents, and life members who have shaped the organization over its hundred-year tenure. The work also addresses more modern challenges faced by the RSA from 1967 to 2003 and its transition into the 21st century. This record is an essential resource for those researching military history, the social history of NZ veterans, or genealogical information related to RSA membership and leadership.  The appendices offer a thorough index of key figures and badges of honor, making it a definitive reference for collectors and historians alike.

title: Murray Ball Cartoon Collection Six of the Best NZ Cultural Icon Comic Art
body:
Six of the Best

Stanley the Caveman Comic Strips
The Prophet Philosophical Cartoons
Bruce the Barbarian Medieval Humour
The Doctor Medical Satire
Nature Calls Animal Kingdom Illustrations
The Kids Childhood Memories Sketches
Foreword by Dog

This substantial hardback anthology, Six of the Best, brings together a diverse range of work from Murray Ball, New Zealand's most celebrated cartoonist. While best known for Footrot Flats, this collection highlights the breadth of his creative output, featuring six distinct series that showcase his signature wit and masterful line work. The volume opens with a foreword by the Dog, providing a familiar entry point into the more varied and sometimes experimental strips contained within. Each section is introduced with personal notes from the creator, offering rare insight into the inspiration and development of these characters.

The collection features Stanley, the "intellectual caveman" and classic no-hoper, whose prehistoric trials serve as a timeless commentary on modern society. This is followed by The Prophet, a strip exploring failed prognostications and philosophical irony. Bruce the Barbarian offers a humorous take on the warrior archetype, famously given an Australian name as a playful nod to trans-Tasman sporting rivalries. The Doctor provides a satirical look at medical professions, born from the artist's own experiences with the healthcare system. Nature Calls focuses on the animal world with heartfelt observational humour, while The Kids looks back at the complexities and joys of childhood with a nostalgic yet unsentimental lens.

Nelson and Sons, serves as a primary example of period adventure literature focusing on the African continent. The text contains sixty-five technical engravings that document various aspects of African wildlife, landscape,  hunting and exploration activities. The content is structured as a detailed account of travel and survival within the interior regions of Africa, providing observations on indigenous fauna and geographical features.The physical production features an illustrated cloth binding characteristic of the era's educational and recreational library sets. 

The narrative components are centered on the logistical challenges and ecological findings of the period, maintaining a focus on the natural history of the African wilds. The engravings are integrated throughout the text 
Condition: Blue cloth boards with ornate black and gold stamped illustrations and title. Significant wear and fraying to the head and tail of the spine. Occasional foxing and spotting to the title page and interior leaves. Binding is intact but shows structural age.
Date: 1898
Pages: 560

title: Whitebaiters Never Lie  Exploring an Iconic Kiwi Culture
body:
A visual celebration of a quintessential New Zealand pastime.

 This book documents and celebrates the characters who catch the most-prized Kiwi food - the whitebait.

 Whitebaiters are a breed apart and photographer Anita Paters and Murray Hedwig have spent over three years travelling the country talking to and photographing New Zealanders about their passion, and documenting the, at times, complex unwritten 'rules' that surround it

- Photographic Journey: With over 460 full-colour images, it captures the rugged charm of whitebaiters across Aotearoa—from remote West Coast rivers to urban wharves in Auckland.
-  Authentic Characters: The book showcases the people behind the nets—those who fiercely guard their “pozzies” and spin tall tales about their catch sizes.
-  Lifestyle & Lore: It documents the cribs, shacks, and shelters, the diverse fishing techniques, and even includes 13 recipes (yes, proper whitebait fritters—no flour!).
-  Cultural Insight: With a foreword by Keri Hulme, it’s not just about fishing—it’s about identity, tradition, and the Kiwi spirit of perseverance and storytelling.



Hardback Good+++

Some wear to the dustjacket - It has some repairs..

Otherwise excellent internally

title: Pioneer Nurse In The Marlborough Sounds  Carol King-Turner 1940's
body:
Pioneer Nurse In The Marlborough Sounds    Carol King-Turner

Take Carol King-Turner, who became the Marlborough Sound's first District Nurse in 1946, based at French Pass. She's not that well known out of Marlborough, but in my eyes she should be a national heroine.   

Not only was she expected to be a superb nurse, experienced midwife too, but she had to handle boats with finicky engines while judging changing weather and tides. No road existed out to French Pass back in the 1940s and 50s, let alone D'Urville Island, so she'd think nothing of clambering up and down steep hillsides to get to isolated homesteads.

This she did all most admirably, not to mention bearing her first two kids, Rossi and James, on the job. Childcare was not an issue, because she would more than often take her kids with her. Those kids recall getting to know every toybox and toilet in the Sounds, whether it was a long drop, bucket or luxury flush. 
Remembered by many older residents of the Western Sounds, Carol will always be the extraordinary nurse who largely alone covered a unique and vast area at such a special time.

title: History of Rongotea  by Pamela Benson
body:
History of Rongotea  by Pamela Benson

Published to co-incide with the centennial of the Rongotea School and covering its 100 years from 1881, Pamela Benson's well researched and comprehensive history also provides an excellent account of the Rongotea settlement and the surrounding district. 

This extends back to the 1840s, when the first Europeans explored the territory, with the first new settlers arriving in the 1870s to take up their holdings. The author has included in the history the early developments, with timber milling important in the first years as agriculture was consolidated. 

Chapters include those dealing with the growth of dairying, the provision of community services such as schools, civic and social amenities, the impact of two world wars and the Great Depression, taking the account up to the early 1980s and describing all significent events in the progress of the district. 

First edition of 1981, 125 pages, 50 b/w photos, three maps/plans. early school book reproductions, chapter end cameos, colour tone pictorial e/papers. 

Final text pages include sources, summary of the centennial celebrations (which includeds 24 b/w photos plus two double page photo montages), list of school committees and teachers, plus index.

title: Wanganui Pioneer Henry C Field C.E 1825 - 1912
body:
Born in Hampshire, England, he arrived in New Zealand in 1851 and eventually settled in Wanganui.

 He was a key figure in the development of regional infrastructure, most notably responsible for the construction of Field’s Track, which provided a vital roading link between Wanganui and Karioi.

Beyond his technical work, he was a published author; one of his most significant works is "The Ferns of New Zealand" (1890), reflecting his deep interest in natural history.

 He retired from active service in 1884

title: Dunedin And Port Chalmers Railway
body:
Dunedin And Port Chalmers Railway 

Dunedin and Port Chalmers Railway. New Zealand's First 3ft. 6in. Gauge Line by T. A. McGavin. 'Although New Zealand's first railways were built 1862 and 1864, it was the end of that decade before serious thoughts were being given to the development of a railway system in a national sense. 


These thoughts were crystallised in Julius Vogel's famous immigration and public works proposals of June 1870, which embodied the adoption of a standard 3ft. 6in. gauge railway for practically all future railway construction in the country. 


Meanwhile, in 1869, plans had been prepared in Dunedin for a 4ft. 8.5in. gauge railway to Port Chalmers, but under the influence of the national proposals it was actually built to the narrower gauge. 

Thus it came about that a provincially inspired railway project became the first in New Zealand to use the new standard of 3ft. 6in. gauge, and ultimately to become an important part of the country's railway system.'




Cardcovered 22 pages 1973

title: NZ's First Railway Dun Mountain Railway Nelson NZ Pioneer Transportation Nelson
body:

This publication examines the development and operation of the Dun Mountain Railway in Nelson, which holds the distinction of being the first railway established in NZ. It covers the mid-19th-century origins of the line, which was primarily constructed to transport minerals from the Dun Mountain mines to the Nelson port. The text provides technical information regarding the horse-drawn nature of the early service, the gauge of the tracks, and the geological challenges faced during construction across the rugged Nelson terrain. It records the transition from a mineral line to a passenger service and its eventual decline. Included are archival illustrations and photographs depicting the early rolling stock, the mountain incline, and the infrastructure utilized during the 1860s. The content references key figures involved in the engineering and financing of the project, as well as the impact of the railway on the economic growth of the Nelson region. It serves as a record of early colonial engineering and transportation within the Tasman district. title: Marlborough Sounds Reserves Handbook Queen Charlotte Pelorus Sound NZ
body:
MARLBOROUGH SOUNDS RESERVES HANDBOOK

This handbook provides a comprehensive guide to the thousands of acres of reserve land set aside for recreation and preservation in the Marlborough Sounds, located at the north-eastern tip of the South Island, NZ. The text explores the complex geographical landscape of forested valleys and waterways, including the major inlets of Queen Charlotte and Pelorus Sounds, D'Urville Island, and the Croisilles. It records the rich history of the region, referencing Maori and pre-Maori occupation alongside early European exploration by figures such as Captain James Cook, who utilized Ship Cove during his voyages in 1770, 1773-4, and 1777. Further historical accounts include the 1820 visit by Russian explorer Thaddeus Bellingshausen and the 1826 arrival of Frenchman Dumont D'Urville. The publication documents early industry, noting the establishment of Port Underwood as a whaling base in 1826 and the 1827 settlement at Te Awaiti on Arapawa Island by John Guard. It features details on the administration of these lands by local Reserves Boards and the development of access roads originally built for isolated farming communities. The content provides a factual summary of the 92 diverse reserves within the 1,125 square mile area, covering land management, timber, and state forests. This resource is ideal for those interested in coastal NZ history, maritime exploration, and regional environmental conservation. The front cover features a photograph of the Captain Cook monument at Ship Cove, flanked by historic cannons.

title: Rangiatea Church in Otaki A History by Taepa, Rev. Canon Hohepa 1851-1963
body:
The Rangiatea Story  by Taepa, Rev. Canon Hohepa


New Zealand Rev Taepa was pastor from 1952-57 and again 1963 to printing of this history  48 pages


Rangiatea Church in Otaki, New Zealand was the oldest Maori Anglican church in New Zealand. Originally completed in 1851, the building was burnt down by an arsonist in 1995, and by 2003 the church had been completely rebuilt
 

In 1848, Te Rauparaha who had just returned to Otaki from Australia, issued the challenge of building the church to the chief of Te Wehiwehi, who accepted.Rangiatea Church was built under the direction of Te Rauparaha and English missionary Octavius Hadfield
During the original construction in the 1840s, large totara logs had to be floated down rivers at nearby Ohau and Waikawa.

 The logs became the ridge pole and the central pillars of the church. The rafters, pillars and slabs were also made from totara
The design of the church is unique in that it incorporates ideas from both English and Maori church design.

body:

Originally presented at the History of Science in NZ Conference in Wellington between 12 and 14 February 1983, the work was prepared and issued by the Botany Division of the DSIR in Christchurch. As a formal conference paper, it provides a specialized look at the development of botanical thought in the region, authored by researchers within the DSIR (Department of Scientific and Industrial Research). The narrative details Cockayne’s influence on the scientific community, his methodologies, and the lasting impact of his ecological studies on the NZ landscape. The Rangitikei Circuit

title: Miranui The story of Largest flax Mill Southern Press Shannon Horowhenua History
body:
Miranui (Maori for "big mill") was a pioneering, large-scale flax mill operating from 1907 to 1933 near Shannon, Horowhenua, and was arguably the largest in the Southern Hemisphere. Owned by the A&L Seifert Flaxdressing Company, it produced up to 2,500 tons of fibre annually and was a major engine of the local economy.

Opened in November 1907 by Alfred and Louis Seifert, it was the first mill specially designed for massive-scale flax fibre production from the Makerua Swamp.Operation: The mill was located 2.5 miles north of Shannon on the Shannon-Tokomaru road (modern Highway 57). It processed Phormium tenax (harakeke) from the 22,000-acre Makerua swamp.Peak Production: By 1916–17, 19 mills operated in the area, with Miranui employing 300 men at its peak. It had seven stripping machines.Industrial Innovation: The mill was famous for its 3ft gauge tramway, which ran 3.5 miles into the swamp to bring flax to the machines.

Today, almost all evidence of the mill is gone, with the swamp converted into dairy farmland.The only remaining trace is the ruins of the scutching shed.The area is still known as the Opiki Plains, and the site of the mill is on the Shannon-Tokomaru road

Author: Bob Ayson.Publisher: Southern Press.Published: 1977.


 This 24-page booklet documents the history of the Seifert brothers' mill, the flax processing method, and the surrounding community

title: NZ HISTORY  Land of the Maniapoto - Rare King Country Historical Record
body:
HISTORY  Land of the Maniapoto - King Country Regional History NZ


This volume provides an account of the "Land of the Maniapoto," focusing on the geographic and historical region commonly known as the King Country (Te Rohe Pōtae). It documents the traditional history of the Ngāti Maniapoto iwi, their ancestral lands, and the subsequent colonial encounters that shaped the district from the mid-19th century through to the early 20th century
It includes details regarding the tribal boundaries of Te Nehenehenui and the political landscape of the King Country during the era of the aukati (the border established after the Waikato War). It chronicles the interactions between Maniapoto leaders—such as Rewi Maniapoto and Wahanui—and the colonial government, particularly concerning the opening of the territory for the Main Trunk Railway and European settlement.

title: Bushwackers - Hunting Adventures In Stewart Island Dean Taylor
body:
Bushwackers - Hunting Adventures In Stewart Island

A the age of 16, Dean Taylor was invited to go on his first hunting party to Stewart Island. It was a trip that immediately captivated him with the rugged beauty of the island, and the thrill of the hunt of deer in such a remote part of the world. 

Since then he's been back to the island another 21 times.

Over the years, Dean gets together a regular hunting party, a group of men who enjoy the chase, the scenery and more than a few beers. The tales are told with humour and capture the comraderie and the isolation that is experienced while hunting in such a remote part of the world. The lads have been chased by sharks, have had numerous close shaves with tree rats and have possibly shot as many deer as they have missed.

 The tally of deer carcasses is only surpassed by the tally of empty beer cans on their return to the mainland. Would you believe that on more than one hunting trip Dean has come across a hippo on Stewart Island?  

July 2009

184 pages Softcovered some wear a couple of pages with light soiling
